title: Two Faces: Exploring the Framework and Implications of Anthropomorphism in the Age of AI
abstract: A growing mass of research looms overhead, raining the praises of the remarkable abilities of AI in mimicking the mannerisms and nuances of human beings. While a select cohort of individuals, specifically capitalists, stand to benefit from this advancement, several others have begun questioning what it is to be human if it can be substituted with such ease. Given this context, this review aims to dichotomise this phenomenon, displaying anthropomorphism and dehumanisation as two sides of the same coin. The review in this paper focuses on the factors that create the optimal environment for the flourishing of anthropomorphic tendencies of new age technology. To this end, neurocapitalism and technofeudalism are discussed to provide context to a society that deems humans to be chasms of data. Emerging phenomena such as using chatbots as a friend/therapist, social robots, and the ethical and social implications of the anthropomorphic qualities of AI are discussed.

title: Green Bond Policies Driving Circular Economy Goals: Potential Gains and Roadblocks
abstract: The fast-paced growth of green bonds has positioned them as an important instrument for financing the transition toward a circular economy (CE). However, empirical evidence on how green bond proceeds are allocated and aligned with CE principles in emerging economies remains limited. This study examines green-bond–financed projects under Series VII A and VII B of the Indian Renewable Energy Development Agency (IREDA) using structured content analysis of ESG reports, annual reports, and sustainability disclosures. The findings show that green bonds effectively mobilize capital for renewable-energy–based CE initiatives, particularly those related to resource efficiency, waste reduction, and sustainable infrastructure. Nevertheless, challenges persist due to fragmented reporting practices, greenwashing concerns, and regulatory inconsistencies. The analysis further indicates that firms with transparent fund allocation strategies enhance investor trust, thereby supporting broader market participation. By providing project-level evidence from India, this study offers policy and investment insights for strengthening the role of green bonds in advancing circular economy transitions.

title: Gender Bias and Governance Evasion in Boards of Indian Family Firms: Challenges and solutions
abstract: The recognition of women’s inheritance rights through the Hindu Succession (Amendment) Act, 2005 and the Companies Act, 2013 has renewed debate on gender equality in Indian family-owned businesses. Despite legal progress, significant barriers to fair succession persist. This paper examines the gap between women’s formal legal rights and their actual participation in family enterprises. It analyses how family trusts, wills, and shareholder agreements often override statutory entitlements, reinforcing male-line succession. This paper identifies the strengths and limitations of current legal reforms and finds that patriarchal structures within business families frequently dilute women’s control. To address these challenges, it proposes stronger enforcement of gender inclusion in corporate governance, greater transparency in family trust structures, and enhanced judicial oversight in succession disputes. Such measures can bridge the gap between statutory equality and actual participation, ensuring more equitable leadership and inheritance outcomes for women in family businesses.

title: Intersecting Inequalities: Mapping Energy Poverty and Women’s Empowerment Across India

author.name: Debalina Chakravarty, Sreeja Patra, Sangita Dutta Gupta, M. Manjula,   Indrani Roychoudhury,  Ramesh S Kampli

abstract: Energy challenges in India are particularly acute, with energy poverty affecting households in multidimensional ways. Limited access to affordable and clean cooking fuels, combined with constrained women’s agency, exacerbates time poverty and health risks and restricts women’s participation in paid employment, undermines women’s empowerment. While existing studies show a close link between energy access and gender outcomes in the Indian context; however, substantial regional disparities persist. This study examines the relationship between household energy access and women’s empowerment in India at national and state levels using data from the National Family Health Survey (NFHS-5). The study employs descriptive statistical methods and the Concentration Index (CI) to assess inequalities in access to clean cooking fuel and electricity and their association with key empowerment indicators. The findings reveal pronounced interstate disparities, with southern and western states showing higher energy access, while central and eastern states continue to lag. Improved energy access is associated with greater women’s decision-making autonomy, higher participation in paid employment, and improved access to financial and digital resources. The results underscore the need for state-specific policies to expand clean energy access in lagging regions and strengthen women’s economic empowerment.

title: Beyond Recipients: A Framework for Meaningful Child Beneficiary Participation in Non-government Organizations
abstract: As the Sustainable Development Goals approach their final years, amplifying the voices of marginalized communities is paramount for inclusive development. This qualitative, multiple-case study sought to understand how child beneficiaries contribute to project management and what factors influence NGOs to engage in participation. A total of 52 respondents participated in the study, including six board trustees, eight managers, and 38 beneficiaries. The research on three NGOs identified four key antecedents of child beneficiary participation: 1) management’s stakeholder perception of beneficiaries; 2) beneficiaries’ self-perception as stakeholders; 3) the mutual trust between leaders and beneficiaries; and 4) a shared desire for beneficiary self-reliance.This study contributes to theory by providing a framework of meaningful participation of child beneficiaries in NGOs. While the findings do not allow for statistical generalization, they offer insights into participatory practices that may be relevant to other NGOs, particularly those operating in high-power distance cultures. It underscores the need for NGO leaders to critically examine their beliefs, values and perspectives, ultimately reinforcing that child beneficiaries are not merely recipients but active agents capable of shaping organizational outcomes.

title: Banaras: City Of Light by Diana L. Eck, Alfred A. Knopf, New York, USA, 1982, ISBN: 0-394-51971-X, Pages-427.

author.name: Ashish Ranjan Tiwari, Ph.D scholar, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi.
